query: Voyager 1 April 2026 instrument shutdown which instrument turned off remaining instruments NASA

{"query":"Voyager 1 April 2026 instrument shutdown which instrument turned off remaining instruments NASA","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1735,"verdict":null,"extra_info":{"type":"featured_snippet","text":"Mission engineers at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California turned off the Low-energy Charged Particles experiment aboard Voyager 1 on April 17, 2026. The LECP has been operating almost without interruption since Voyager 1 launched in 1977 — almost 49 years.Apr 17, 2026"},"results":[{"url":"https://science.nasa.gov/blogs/voyager/2026/04/17/nasa-shuts-off-instrument-on-voyager-1-to-keep-spacecraft-operating/#:~:text=Mission%20engineers%20at%20NASA's%20Jet,1%20on%20April%2017%2C%202026.&text=The%20LECP%20has%20been%20operating,in%201977%20%E2%80%94%20almost%2049%20years.","title":"NASA Shuts Off Instrument on Voyager 1 to Keep Spacecraft Operating - NASA Science","content":"The LECP has been operating almost without interruption since Voyager 1 launched in 1977 — almost 49 years.
Mission engineers knew any additional drop in power could trigger the spacecraft’s undervoltage fault protection system, which would shut down components on its own to safeguard the probe, requiring recovery by the flight team — a lengthy process that carries its own risks.
“Voyager 1 still has two remaining operating science instruments — one that listens to plasma waves and one that measures magnetic fields."},{"url":"https://www.universetoday.com/articles/another-instrument-shut-down-on-voyager-1-to-extend-its-interstellar-mission","title":"Another Instrument Shut Down on Voyager 1 to Extend its Interstellar Mission - Universe Today","content":"After nearly 50 years of continuous operation, these generators are running low on power.
Engineers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) sent commands to shut down the instrument on April 17th, following the shutdown of the Cosmic Ray Subsystem (CRS) in February 2025 and the imaging system, ultraviolet spectrometer, and other instruments since 1990.
*NASA’s Voyager 2 spacecraft entered interstellar space in November 2018, more than six years after its twin, Voyager 1, did the same.
The decision was made after *Voyager 1* experienced an unexpected drop in power on Feb.
For Voyager 1, the LECP was next on the list for Voyager 1*, which had already been shut off on Voyager 2* back in March 2025.
In the meantime, the engineers are taking the time this provides to finalize a larger strategy to extend the mission, which they call the "Big Bang." The plan involves turning off a group of powered devices simultaneously and activating low-power alternatives to keep the spacecraft warm enough to continue gathering science data.
The team will implement the Big Bang on *Voyager 2* first since it has slightly more power and is closer to Earth."},{"url":"https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260504023835.htm","title":"NASA shuts down 49-year-old Voyager 1 instrument to keep it alive | ScienceDaily","content":"The spacecraft is running critically low on energy, forcing NASA to make careful sacrifices to keep its mission going.
Each Voyager originally carried 10 sets of instruments, and seven have already been turned off.
Because Voyager 1 is now more than 15 billion miles (25 billion kilometers) from Earth, communication takes time.
Tests are scheduled for May and June 2026.
"NASA shuts down 49-year-old Voyager 1 instrument to keep it alive." ScienceDaily. www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260504023835.htm (accessed July 15, 2026)."},{"url":"https://satnews.com/2026/04/19/nasa-shuts-off-instrument-on-voyager-1-to-extend-interstellar-mission/","title":"NASA Shuts Off Instrument on Voyager 1 to Extend Interstellar Mission – SatNews","content":"On Friday, April 17, 2026, engineers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) sent commands to deactivate one of the few remaining science instruments aboard Voyager 1.
To stay ahead of this threshold, engineers chose to manually retire the LECP, which had been operating almost continuously since the probe’s launch in 1977.
The shutdown of the LECP provides Voyager 1 with roughly one year of “breathing room.” During this time, the team is finalizing a high-stakes power management plan internally known as the Big Bang.
If successful, the same fix will be applied to Voyager 1 no sooner than July 2026.
On November 15, 2026, the spacecraft is projected to reach a symbolic distance of exactly one light-day from Earth—approximately 16.1 billion miles (25.9 billion km)."}]}

tavily.raw: {"query":"Voyager 1 April 2026 instrument shutdown which instrument turned off remaining instruments NASA","follow_up_questions":null,"answer":null,"images":[],"results":[{"url":"https://www.facebook.com/spacecom/posts/nasa-engineers-have-shut-down-another-instrument-on-voyager-1-to-preserve-the-po/1311773324146682","title":"Voyager 1 instrument shut down to conserve power","content":"On April 17, 2026, NASA switched off one of Voyager 1's science instruments — the LECP — after it had been running almost nonstop since 1977.","score":0.8995547,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260504023835.htm","title":"NASA shuts down 49-year-old Voyager 1 instrument to ...","content":":   Voyager 1 just powered down a nearly 50-year-old instrument to stay alive in deep space. The spacecraft is running critically low on energy, forcing NASA to make careful sacrifices to keep its mission going. Engineers are now working on a bold plan that could extend its life — and possibly revive the instrument later. On April 17, engineers at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) in Southern California sent a command to turn off an instrument on Voyager 1 known as the Low-energy Charged Particles experiment, or LECP. The spacecraft, powered by a nuclear energy source, is gradually losing power, and shutting down this instrument is the most effective way to extend the life of humanity's first mission into interstellar space. Voyager 1 and Voyager 2 remain the only spacecraft far enough from Earth to collect this kind of data. If the plan works as hoped, there is even a possibility that Voyager 1's LECP instrument could be turned back on in the future.","score":0.8945781,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.howtogeek.com/nasa-shuts-down-major-voyager-instruments","title":"NASA Shuts Down Major Voyager Instruments, But for Good Reason","content":"An artistic depiction of NASA’s Voyager 1 spacecraft traveling through interstellar space. Prior to starting with How-to Geek in January 2024, he worked as a document producer, data manager, and content creator for over ten years, and loves making spreadsheets and documents in his spare time. He has worked as a data manager in a secondary school in the UK and has years of experience in the classroom with Microsoft PowerPoint. NASA has confirmed it is turning off key instruments on Voyager 1 and Voyager 2, leaving just three science instruments in operation on each spacelab. Patrick Koehn, Voyager program scientist at NASA Headquarters in Washington, said, \"Every bit of additional data we have gathered since [the spacecraft completed their mission to study the outer planets] is not only valuable bonus science for heliophysics, but also a testament to the exemplary engineering that has gone into the Voyagers—starting nearly 50 years ago and continuing to this day.\".","score":0.769306,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://gizmodo.com/nasa-shut-down-voyager-science-instrument-after-power-levels-fell-unexpectedly-2000748707","title":"NASA Shut Down Voyager 1 Science Instrument After Unexpected Power Drop","content":"Engineers are working on a long-term plan to keep the iconic spacecraft alive. An artist's concept of the Voyager 1 spacecraft. NASA engineers are working to keep the Voyager mission alive as it cruises through interstellar space, opting to shut down components of the spacecraft to save power. Engineers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) sent commands to Voyager 1 to shut off one of its science instruments after the spacecraft’s power levels fell unexpectedly. Out of the 10 instruments on board Voyager 1, only two are still operating as the mission team figures out new ways to keep the spacecraft alive for longer. “While shutting down a science instrument is not anybody’s preference, it is the best option available,” Kareem Badaruddin, Voyager mission manager at JPL, said in a statement. The journey, however, has taken a toll on the spacecraft, and NASA engineers are forced to come up with new ways to extend the mission.","score":0.76173395,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.jpl.nasa.gov/news/nasa-shuts-off-instrument-on-voyager-1-to-keep-spacecraft-operating","title":"NASA Shuts Off Instrument on Voyager 1 to Keep Spacecraft Operating | NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL)","content":"[](https://www.jpl.nasa.gov/news/nasa-shuts-off-instrument-on-voyager-1-to-keep-spacecraft-operating).
